title: A Simple and Rapid System for DNA and RNA Isolation from Diverse Plants Using Handmade Kit
author: Farhad Masoomi-Aladizgeh
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.21203/rs.2.1347/v2

steps.title: Add CTAB (0
steps.instruction: Add CTAB (0.5 g), EDTA (1 g), Tris base (2.5 g), and NaCl (5 g) as tetrad components of the extraction buffer to 100 ml autoclaved water and then gradually dissolve it by shaking at room temperature. CRITICAL Add 10 µl βME to 1 ml of the extraction buffer before using to decrease the possible oxidation only for tissues with high polysaccharides and secondary metabolites. CRITICAL Add 15 mg PVPP per 1 ml of extraction buffer only for tissues with high polyphenolic pollution.

steps.title: Ground samples (leaf, shoot, root, and recalcitrant samples, approximately 0
steps.instruction: Ground samples (leaf, shoot, root, and recalcitrant samples, approximately 0.5-1 g) using 1 ml of the extraction buffer with or without liquid nitrogen in mortar and pestle that are sterilized. CRITICAL STEP The procedure are carried out at room temperature except the centrifugation steps (at 4℃) as well as the time of precipitating of the nucleic acid using the isopropanol (on ice). CRITICAL STEP Severely disrupt the tissues to create the glaze mode of samples. TIMING: 10 min for 5 samples

steps.title: Transfer the resulting solution to a...
steps.instruction: Transfer the resulting solution to a sterile centrifuge tube (size=2 ml), and then mix sample by briefly vortexing until the sample is thoroughly resuspended.

steps.title: In this step, incubate samples at...
steps.instruction: In this step, incubate samples at 65℃ for 10 min for lysing cells completely.

steps.title: Add 600 µl of chloroform
steps.instruction: Add 600 µl of chloroform: isoamyl alcohols (24:1) to each tube, homogenize them by vortexing.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 13700 g at 4℃ for 10 min
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 13700 g at 4℃ for 10 min. CRITICAL STEP The PH of the extraction buffer is about 8-9, resulting in DNA and RNA precipitation, however, it could result in lower DNA and higher RNA concentrations in case of reducing the PH to around 6-7.

steps.title: Transfer the upper aqueous phase to...
steps.instruction: Transfer the upper aqueous phase to a new tube (size= 1.5 ml).

steps.title: Add 700 µl of cold isopropanol...
steps.instruction: Add 700 µl of cold isopropanol to precipitate RNA or DNA and then invert tubes 3-4 times to mix the solution.

steps.title: Centrifuge tubes at 13700 g at 4℃ for 10 min
steps.instruction: Centrifuge tubes at 13700 g at 4℃ for 10 min. The white pellet will be visible on the bottom of the tubes. CRITICAL STEP Do not disturb bottom phases of the solution when you pipet the upper phase.

steps.title: Discard the supernatant and wash the...
steps.instruction: Discard the supernatant and wash the precipitate nucleic acid gently with 70% EtOH.

steps.title: After centrifuging at 5400 g at...
steps.instruction: After centrifuging at 5400 g at 4˚C for 5 min, remove the supernatant and then air-dry the pellet.

steps.title: Dissolve the pellet in 20-30 µl...
steps.instruction: Dissolve the pellet in 20-30 µl of RNase free water (commercial) or autoclaved water.

steps.title: After incubating at room temperature for...
steps.instruction: After incubating at room temperature for a few minutes, keep the solubilized nucleic acid in −20˚C for a short time storage or in −80˚C for a longtime storage. Figure 1 shows a schematic model of all the DNA and RNA isolation steps of this procedure.

steps.title: TIMING Tissue grinding, Steps 2-3
steps.instruction: TIMING Tissue grinding, Steps 2-3: 10 min Isolation of nucleic acid, Steps 4-6: 25 min Nucleic acid precipitation, Steps 7-9: 15 min Preparation and dissolving the nucleic acid, Steps 10-13: 10 min

steps.title: Troubleshooting advice can be found in Figure 2
steps.instruction: Troubleshooting advice can be found in Figure 2.

steps.title: The main purpose of this research...
steps.instruction: The main purpose of this research was to access a simple protocol by which nucleic acid isolation would be possible for all plants, and relevant projects can be performed at each laboratory with having access to general chemical resources. Dual applications of the extraction buffer is resulted in both DNA and RNA (shown in Figure 3). DNA and RNA can be purified by adding RNase and DNase enzymes, respectively, or by adjusting the PH of the extraction buffer. It is considerable that all the buffer components consist certain amount of the chemicals that is one of the big advantages of this protocol. PCR, RT-PCR, real time-PCR and southern blot analysis have been conducted to approve the quality of the extracted DNA and RNA which are acceptable (shown in Figure 4).

steps.instruction: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interests

steps.title: Figure1
steps.instruction: Figure1. Figure 1 Schematic model of all the DNA and RNA isolation steps Figure2. Table 1 TROUBLESHOOTING Figure2. Figure 2 DNA and RNA quality from isolated samples Figure3. Figure 3 PCR, RT-PCR, real time-PCR and southern blot analysis
